# Abstract

This review addresses common questions regarding the role of surgical lung biopsy in the diagnosis and treatment of interstitial lung disease (ILD). We specifically address when a surgical lung biopsy (SLB) can be diagnostic as well as when it may be avoided, e.g., when the combination of the clinical context and the imaging pattern seen on high resolution CT (HRCT) chest can provide a confident diagnosis. Existing studies on the diagnostic utility as well as the complications associated with SLB are reviewed; as are the performance characteristics and reliability of HRCT scan of the chest in predicting the underlying histopathology of the lung. The review is formatted in the form of answers to questions that clinicians regularly ask when considering SLB in a patient with ILD.

# When can the combination of the clinical context and chest imaging pattern on HRCT provide a confident diagnosis?

The importance of a comprehensive history and physical examination cannot be overemphasized, as a thorough history and physical examination defines the clinical context and generates the most important data in evaluating patients with ILD (Figure 1)3,4. Details of what constitutes a complete history, physical examination and appropriate non-invasive diagnostic testing as it pertains to the evaluation of a patient with ILD are beyond the scope of this review and can be obtained elsewhere, but the history, physical examination and non-invasive testing should focus on the severity and temporal behavior of symptoms, presence of pre-existing systemic conditions known to be associated with the development of ILD, particularly signs and symptoms of any extra-pulmonary disease, the presence of a family history of lung disease, and the exposure to substances known to result in pulmonary injury3,4. Pulmonary physiology complements the history and physical exam in providing a measure of impairment, and establishing a baseline against which to assess future disease activity or response to therapy.

When available, the temporal behavior of the disease in terms of signs and symptoms, clinical variability, radiologic progression vs. stability, and responsiveness to any previous intervention can all add important information.

A HRCT chest is the next investigation, and all patients with suspected ILD should undergo one (Figure 1). In order for the test to provide useful information, careful attention to CT technique is critical5. Appropriate technique will include contiguous thin sections and multiplanar reconstructions. Expiratory and prone images often provide important information.

and should be considered in all patients. In particular, the imaging technologists must ensure that

inspiration is adequate, and that respiratory motion is absent. Previous chest imaging, when

available, should be reviewed and compared with more recent imaging to assess for evolution of

the interstitial process. With these caveats,     MANUS CRIP T
                                                  many ILDs are associated with HRCT patterns that

significantly narrow the differential ⁶⁻⁹. However, there is a substantial learning curve associated

with the interpretation of these images, and dedicated thoracic radiologists will have a lower

inter-observer variation than general radiologists ¹⁰. Similar to radiologists, the performance of

clinicians, with or without HRCT information, varies with the experience and expertise¹¹.

The pattern seen on HRCT of the chest provides critical information, can often predict

the underlying histologic pattern and is defined by the combination of the radiologic features and

their distribution. A UIP pattern is defined as peripheral and basal predominant disease

characterized by the presence of reticular abnormalities, honeycombing (clustered cystic
ACCEP TED
airspaces of comparable diameters, characterized by well-defined walls, usually subpleural, and

3-10 mm in diameter), and the absence of a number of features such as extensive ground glass

abnormality, profuse micronodules, discrete cysts, diffuse mosaic attenuation or air trapping, and

consolidation (Figures 2a, 2b) ¹. When a trained thoracic radiologist has a high level of

confidence in the presence of a definite UIP pattern, the findings are specific (but not sensitive)

for the presence of a histopathologic UIP pattern on SLB (e-Table 1, e-Table 2, online

supplemental material). When this pattern is seen in the clinical context of an idiopathic

interstitial pneumonia (IIP), these findings are diagnostic of IPF and a SLB is not needed. For

example, more than half the patients enrolled in recent IPF clinical trials were diagnosed based

on the combination of the clinical context and HRCT pattern alone (e-Table 3 Online

supplemental material).

HRCT patterns other than ‘definite UIP pattern’ (Examples: Figures 3a, 3b, 4a, 4b, 5a,

5b) are usually neither sensitive, nor specific enough to preclude the need for a surgical lung

biopsy when a definitive histopathologic pattern diagnosis is necessary (e-Table 2, online

supplemental material). An exception to     MANUS CRIP T
                                            this general rule may be made when the CT features are

otherwise typical for UIP, but honeycombing is absent: in this situation, the predictive value for

UIP is over 80%¹², and may be regarded as diagnostic of histologic UIP in subjects with a high

pretest clinical suspicion of IPF, particularly in those aged over 65 in whom the pretest

probability of IPF is already high¹³. Common competing chest imaging patterns include

nonspecific interstitial pneumonia (NSIP) and fibrotic forms of hypersensitivity pneumonitis,

though, patterns suggestive of sarcoidosis, and organizing pneumonia (OP) have all been

described in patients with histologic UIP ¹⁴⁻¹⁶. A UIP pattern can be particularly hard to

distinguish from NSIP; a significant proportion of HRCTs with an NSIP imaging pattern will
ACCEP TED
have a UIP histologic pattern 14,17-21. Distinguishing between NSIP and UIP patterns on HRCT is

made even more difficult because over time, the chest imaging pattern in a subgroup of patients

with NSIP may evolve to suggest UIP 22,23.

When studied under controlled conditions, the intra- and inter-observer agreement on

HRCT pattern can vary considerably, depending upon the study design, experience and expertise

of the interpreting radiologists, and the heterogeneity of the diagnoses in the patient population

studied (e-Table 2, online supplemental material). This subjectivity in the interpretation of the

HRCT findings is important to recognize, especially in cases where the HRCT pattern diagnosis

is made with low confidence, or if the HRCT findings are discordant with the clinical context.

As specific chest imaging patterns are often seen in particular clinical contexts, a number

of disorders may sometimes be diagnosed with confidence without the need to obtain lung tissue,

HRCT patterns other than ‘definite UIP pattern’ (Examples: Figures 3a, 3b, 4a, 4b, 5a, 5b) are usually neither sensitive, nor specific enough to preclude the need for a surgical lung biopsy when a definitive histopathologic pattern diagnosis is necessary (e-Table 2, online supplemental material). An exception to this general rule may be made when the CT features are otherwise typical for UIP, but honeycombing is absent: in this situation, the predictive value for UIP is over 80%¹², and may be regarded as diagnostic of histologic UIP in subjects with a high pretest clinical suspicion of IPF, particularly in those aged over 65 in whom the pretest probability of IPF is already high¹³. Common competing chest imaging patterns include nonspecific interstitial pneumonia (NSIP) and fibrotic forms of hypersensitivity pneumonitis, though, patterns suggestive of sarcoidosis, and organizing pneumonia (OP) have all been described in patients with histologic UIP¹⁴⁻¹⁶. A UIP pattern can be particularly hard to distinguish from NSIP; a significant proportion of HRCTs with an NSIP imaging pattern will have a UIP histologic pattern14,17-21. Distinguishing between NSIP and UIP patterns on HRCT is made even more difficult because over time, the chest imaging pattern in a subgroup of patients with NSIP may evolve to suggest UIP22,23.

When studied under controlled conditions, the intra- and inter-observer agreement on HRCT pattern can vary considerably, depending upon the study design, experience and expertise of the interpreting radiologists, and the heterogeneity of the diagnoses in the patient population studied (e-Table 2, online supplemental material). This subjectivity in the interpretation of the HRCT findings is important to recognize, especially in cases where the HRCT pattern diagnosis is made with low confidence, or if the HRCT findings are discordant with the clinical context.

As specific chest imaging patterns are often seen in particular clinical contexts, a number of disorders may sometimes be diagnosed with confidence without the need to obtain lung tissue,

6

items.md: HRCT patterns other than ‘definite UIP pattern’ (Examples: Figures 3a, 3b, 4a, 4b, 5a, 5b) are usually neither sensitive, nor specific enough to preclude the need for a surgical lung biopsy when a definitive histopathologic pattern diagnosis is necessary (e-Table 2, online supplemental material). An exception to this general rule may be made when the CT features are otherwise typical for UIP, but honeycombing is absent: in this situation, the predictive value for UIP is over 80%¹², and may be regarded as diagnostic of histologic UIP in subjects with a high pretest clinical suspicion of IPF, particularly in those aged over 65 in whom the pretest probability of IPF is already high¹³.                           of histologic UIP is needed for a definitive diagnosis of IPF, tissue

obtained by transbronchial biopsy will be inadequate. Trans-bronchial biopsies cannot be used to

make a conclusive histopathologic diagnosis of UIP because the UIP histopathologic pattern

depends upon the identification of specific low-power microscopic findings such as areas of

normal lung alternating with areas of patchy fibrosis and microscopic honeycombing. These

changes can only be fully appreciated on a SLB (Figure 6a, 6b) ³¹. Individual features that can be

seen on transbronchial biopsy such as fibroblast foci are not specific for UIP ³¹, and while in

retrospective unblinded studies of patients with IPF, varying combinations of UIP

histopathologic features including fibroblast foci and honeycombing have been demonstrated in

up to one third ³²⁻³⁴ of transbronchial biopsies, there are no prospective studies utilizing an

acceptable    ACCEP TED
gold standard demonstrating that transbronchial biopsies can be confidently used to

diagnose IPF.

The current IPF consensus statement is silent on the diagnostic fate of patients who do

not have a UIP pattern on HRCT and cannot, or choose not to, undergo a SLB¹. In these patients,

bronchoscopy may provide useful information and the previous IPF statement offers guidance

for assigning a probable diagnosis to these patients³⁵. Age and extent of fibrosis on imaging are

predictive of IPF in patients 65 and older even in the absence of honeycombing as long as there

are no clinical or radiologic findings to suggest an alternative diagnosis ¹³.

IPF30. In all cases, if the longitudinal behavior of the patient’s disease differs from the course predicted by the original diagnosis, the need for a SLB should be reconsidered 24,27-29.

If the identification of histologic UIP is needed for a definitive diagnosis of IPF, tissue obtained by transbronchial biopsy will be inadequate. Trans-bronchial biopsies cannot be used to make a conclusive histopathologic diagnosis of UIP because the UIP histopathologic pattern depends upon the identification of specific low-power microscopic findings such as areas of normal lung alternating with areas of patchy fibrosis and microscopic honeycombing. These changes can only be fully appreciated on a SLB (Figure 6a, 6b) 31. Individual features that can be seen on transbronchial biopsy such as fibroblast foci are not specific for UIP 31, and while in retrospective unblinded studies of patients with IPF, varying combinations of UIP histopathologic features including fibroblast foci and honeycombing have been demonstrated in up to one third 32-34 of transbronchial biopsies, there are no prospective studies utilizing an acceptable gold standard demonstrating that transbronchial biopsies can be confidently used to diagnose IPF.

The current IPF consensus statement is silent on the diagnostic fate of patients who do not have a UIP pattern on HRCT and cannot, or choose not to, undergo a SLB1. In these patients, bronchoscopy may provide useful information and the previous IPF statement offers guidance for assigning a probable diagnosis to these patients35. Age and extent of fibrosis on imaging are predictive of IPF in patients 65 and older even in the absence of honeycombing as long as there are no clinical or radiologic findings to suggest an alternative diagnosis 13.

Should a surgical lung biopsy be performed in patients with acute respiratory failure and a previously undiagnosed ILD?

Patients with previously undiagnosed ILD may present with acute respiratory failure.

This group includes patients with previously unrecognized ILD presenting with an acute

exacerbation, and patients with previously recognized ILD but in whom the evaluation was

deferred due to patient or physician preference.   MANUS CRIP T

The vast majority of SLB data in patients with respiratory failure comes from patients

with ARDS or those that were immunocompromised with pulmonary infiltrates (e-Table 4,

online supplemental material), with infection and malignancy the most common specific

diagnoses in these series. Diffusely metastatic pulmonary malignancy can usually be diagnosed

with TBBX. Bronchoalveolar lavage identifies the majority of pulmonary infections with high

sensitivity and negative predictive value and has performance characteristics comparable to that

of surgical lung biopsy 36,37. The majority of non-infectious and non-malignant diagnoses in

these series are described using nonspecific terms such as fibrosis, nonspecific pneumonitis,
ACCEP TED
chronic pneumonitis, interstitial fibrosis, interstitial pneumonitis, interstitial pulmonary fibrosis

etc., none of which provide a specific histologic pattern diagnosis.

All fibrotic interstitial lung diseases including IPF, CTD-associated UIP or NSIP,

Idiopathic NSIP, chronic hypersensitivity pneumonitis, DIP and asbestosis have been associated

with acute exacerbations and respiratory failure. Patients with acute exacerbations of fibrotic

interstitial lung disease generally show diffuse alveolar damage or organizing pneumonia

superimposed on an underlying fibrotic pattern, and this underlying fibrotic pattern can often be

difficult or impossible to define because of the superimposed acute findings 38,39. Additionally, it

is unclear how a SLB alters therapeutic decision-making in these patients as they are often

treated similarly regardless of biopsy results and have a generally poor prognosis 38,40-45.

Therefore, a SLB is generally not recommended in patients with a newly diagnosed fibrosing

ILD who present in respiratory failure as the morbidity and mortality is relatively high, and the procedure only rarely provides clinically actionable information that cannot be obtained by other, less invasive means. Exceptions might include patients with unusual clinical course or if HRCT pattern suggests an unusual infection or malignancy such as lymphoma which cannot be diagnosed by any other means.

# Should a surgical lung biopsy be performed in patients with connective tissue disease-associated interstitial lung disease?

The ability of HRCT to predict the underlying histopathologic pattern is maintained in CTD. A UIP chest imaging pattern on HRCT predicts the presence of a UIP histopathologic pattern in patients with rheumatoid arthritis associated ILD (RA-ILD) with a high degree of specificity (96%) & positive predictive value (95%)46. The association between the underlying histopathologic pattern and clinical outcomes in patients with CTD is less certain than that seen in the IIPs. For example, in some studies UIP histopathologic pattern on SLB is associated with a worse prognosis in patients with RA-ILD47,48 and scleroderma49, but other studies conclude that the extent of disease on HRCT is the primary driver of outcome50-52. No difference in outcomes between NSIP and UIP was noted in a patient series with various CTD53. A recent model incorporating demographic and physiologic variables predicts prognosis in patients with fibrosing CTD-ILD without the need for biopsy (Gender, Age, and Physiology [GAP] index)54.

In addition, there are no studies in patients with CTD ILD that have used the histopathologic pattern to prospectively direct therapy, and in a retrospective cohort study, the response to immunosuppressive therapy appeared similar across histologic subtypes55.

As a general rule, when the chest imaging pattern seen on HRCT is consistent with what would be expected in a specific CTD, a surgical lung biopsy does not provide additional

What is the ideal biopsy site, number and size?

        The preoperative HRCT chest should be used as a guide to the site of biopsy 4,56. A

TBBX may be preferable to a SLB if most of the abnormality on HRCT is centrally located,

distant from the pleura. Regions of lung most intensely affected by fibrosis, particularly areas of

honeycomb change, should generally be avoided because biopsy from these sites often reveals

end stage fibrosis with only modest diagnostic value ⁵⁷. The site of biopsy should be focused on

involved lung parenchyma or areas adjacent to obviously abnormal lung ⁴. Older literature

cautions against biopsy from the lingula but this has not been borne out in more recent studies ⁵⁶⁻

58.            ACCEP TED                                           3

        Although not prospectively studied, a specimen size of at least 3x2x1 cm appears to be

adequate ⁵⁶. No fewer than two biopsy specimens from separate lobes should be obtained to

address the possibility of discordant histopathologic findings (e.g.,, explanted lungs in patients

with UIP can show areas of NSIP in up to 80% of cases) ⁵⁹. Prospective studies have documented

NSIP pattern in one lobe, and UIP pattern in the other in approximately 25% of all UIP cases

60,61. Sending surgical biopsy specimens routinely for microbiology and cultures in patients with

ILD is generally not necessary ⁶² and this testing should be dictated by the specific clinical

circumstances.

# What is the ideal biopsy site, number and size?

The preoperative HRCT chest should be used as a guide to the site of biopsy 4,56. A TBBX may be preferable to a SLB if most of the abnormality on HRCT is centrally located, distant from the pleura. Regions of lung most intensely affected by fibrosis, particularly areas of honeycomb change, should generally be avoided because biopsy from these sites often reveals end stage fibrosis with only modest diagnostic value 57. The site of biopsy should be focused on involved lung parenchyma or areas adjacent to obviously abnormal lung 4. Older literature cautions against biopsy from the lingula but this has not been borne out in more recent studies 56-58.

Although not prospectively studied, a specimen size of at least 3x2x1 cm appears to be adequate 56. No fewer than two biopsy specimens from separate lobes should be obtained to address the possibility of discordant histopathologic findings (e.g., explanted lungs in patients with UIP can show areas of NSIP in up to 80% of cases) 59. Prospective studies have documented NSIP pattern in one lobe, and UIP pattern in the other in approximately 25% of all UIP cases 60,61. Sending surgical biopsy specimens routinely for microbiology and cultures in patients with ILD is generally not necessary 62 and this testing should be dictated by the specific clinical circumstances.

ACCEPTED MANUSCRIPT



The average in-hospital mortality for patients undergoing SLB in the US from 2000-2011

was 1.7% for elective procedures and 16% for non-elective procedures⁶³. Reported mortality

after SLB in individual series (e-Table 4, e-Figure 1, online supplemental material) varies from

0%⁶⁴ to as high as 70%⁶⁵                    MANUS CRIP T
                            and even in series with high mortality, the mortality is seldom

attributed to the SLB procedure itself⁶⁵. Post SLB mortality is best viewed as a number that

varies more than 100 fold depending on specific risk factors; therefore the actual risk for an

individual patient can be substantially higher or lower than the average mortality rate⁶⁶.

In general, patients with more severe disease and significant co-morbidities are more

likely to die after SLB. Mortality appears higher in patients with hypoxemic respiratory failure,

physiologically severe or rapidly progressive disease, clinically significant comorbidities,

malignancy and the immunosuppressed (e-Table 4, e-Figure 1, online supplemental material).

The series reporting no or low mortality differed from series reporting higher mortality in that

they included   ACCEP TED
few or no patients with severe hypoxia, poor functional status, severe physiologic

restriction, rapidly progressive/accelerated ILD, respiratory failure, malignancy, immune

compromise or multi-system organ failure⁶⁶.

Hypoxemic patients are at particular risk for complications and the severity of hypoxia

directly correlates with worse outcomes 67,68, with mortality increasing in tandem with

progressively increasing hypoxia (one series reported a mortality of 4.2% for patients without

hypoxemia, 6% for patients on supplemental oxygen, 60% for patients with severe hypoxia and

respiratory distress, and 75% for patients who were receiving invasive mechanical ventilation⁶⁹).

Patients in respiratory failure are significantly more likely to die postoperatively than those

without respiratory failure (47.3% vs. 2.2% in one series ⁶⁷, and 100% vs. 4.8% another ⁶⁸).

How should the data obtained from a surgical lung biopsy be used in forming a final

diagnosis?   ACCEP TED

The histopathologic pattern obtained by SLB must be combined with the clinical context

and the chest imaging pattern seen on HRCT to provide a final diagnosis (Figures 6, 7, 8, 9). The

histologic data has a significant influence on the final consensus diagnosis in ILD ², especially

when the combination of the clinical context and chest imaging data produce an uncertain

diagnosis, and when the final diagnosis is not IPF 2,72. There is increasing awareness that a

substantial proportion of subjects who have histologic UIP have CT appearances which would be

regarded as “inconsistent” with UIP⁷³. Such discordance of CT and histologic findings should

always prompt careful review, as well as a renewed search for alternative diagnoses such as

hypersensitivity pneumonitis.

However, the histopathologic pattern seen on surgical lung biopsy does not, in itself,

provide a final diagnosis. The same histopathologic pattern can be seen in a variety of ILDs,

while some ILDs can present with more than one histopathologic pattern. For example, CTD-

associated ILD, hypersensitivity pneumonitis and drug induced ILD can all present with a variety

of histopathologic patterns including diffuse alveolar damage, organizing pneumonia, NSIP or

UIP patterns⁷⁴.     MANUS CRIP T

There is also an element of subjectivity when it comes to interpreting histopathology (e-

Table 5, Online supplemental material), and this subjectivity is important to recognize in cases

where the findings are discordant with clinical and chest imaging data. Diagnostic confidence,

the proportion of correct diagnoses, and agreement on the final diagnosis progressively increases

as clinicians, radiologists and pathologists share and discuss the available clinical, radiologic and

histopathologic data and reach a best fit consensus diagnosis, and the agreement on the final

diagnosis is significantly better than when histopathologic information is interpreted in

isolation². This multidisciplinary approach is now considered to be the gold standard and is the
                    ACCEP TED    1
recommended standard of care for the diagnosis of interstitial lung diseases .

Comparisons of SLB with explanted lungs have shown that up to 10% of surgical

specimens can be non-diagnostic despite being technically adequate⁵⁹. Prospective studies have

shown that a histopathologic diagnosis can be uncertain in up to 18% of ILD cases⁷⁵.

Furthermore, a definitive pathologic pattern diagnosis is not always possible despite a technically

adequate SLB and a multidisciplinary discussion because of conflicting clinical, radiologic or

histopathologic findings which cannot be reconciled; reported prevalence of uncertain diagnosis

varies from 2%- 20% in various series 2,72,76,77. For these patients it is reasonable to provide a

best-fit working diagnosis. Factors to consider include degree of physiologic limitation,

predominant radiologic pattern (e.g., ground glass vs fibrotic) and its extent, predominant

histopathologic pattern (e.g., cellular vs fibrotic), clinical behavior, and response to empiric

longitudinal behavior of the disease with the intent of revising the diagnosis if the clinical course

Conclusion     MANUS CRIP T

When confronted with a patient with suspected ILD, the combination of the clinical

context and chest imaging pattern on HRCT may provide a confident diagnosis without the need

for additional evaluation. The addition of BAL and TBBX to the clinical and imaging data can

be diagnostic in a subset of others. In appropriately selected patients, SLB provides valuable

diagnostic information that cannot be obtained by any other means with low risk of

complication. However, SLB is unnecessary when a confident UIP chest imaging pattern is seen

on HRCT in the clinical context of an IIP, nor is it routinely regularly needed in patients with a

well-defined CTD-ILD and those with a specific, clinically significant, and temporally
               ACCEP TED
appropriate drug, occupational, avocational or accidental exposures. SLB is generally unhelpful

in patients with acute respiratory failure and a new diagnosis of fibrosing ILD. Severe hypoxia,

respiratory failure, immunocompromised status and rapidly progressive disease prior to biopsy

are associated with increased morbidity and mortality. Multidisciplinary discussion, utilizing the

available clinical, HRCT and SLB data is useful in making a definitive diagnosis; however,

diagnostic uncertainty may still occur in a minority of ILD patients.

# Conclusion

When confronted with a patient with suspected ILD, the combination of the clinical context and chest imaging pattern on HRCT may provide a confident diagnosis without the need for additional evaluation. The addition of BAL and TBBX to the clinical and imaging data can be diagnostic in a subset of others. In appropriately selected patients, SLB provides valuable diagnostic information that cannot be obtained by any other means with low risk of complication. However, SLB is unnecessary when a confident UIP chest imaging pattern is seen on HRCT in the clinical context of an IIP, nor is it routinely regularly needed in patients with a well-defined CTD-ILD and those with a specific, clinically significant, and temporally appropriate drug, occupational, avocational or accidental exposures. SLB is generally unhelpful in patients with acute respiratory failure and a new diagnosis of fibrosing ILD. Severe hypoxia, respiratory failure, immunocompromised status and rapidly progressive disease prior to biopsy are associated with increased morbidity and mortality. Multidisciplinary discussion, utilizing the available clinical, HRCT and SLB data is useful in making a definitive diagnosis; however, diagnostic uncertainty may still occur in a minority of ILD patients.

The addition of BAL and TBBX to the clinical and imaging data can be diagnostic in a subset of others. In appropriately selected patients, SLB provides valuable diagnostic information that cannot be obtained by any other means with low risk of complication. However, SLB is unnecessary when a confident UIP chest imaging pattern is seen on HRCT in the clinical context of an IIP, nor is it routinely regularly needed in patients with a well-defined CTD-ILD and those with a specific, clinically significant, and temporally appropriate drug, occupational, avocational or accidental exposures. SLB is generally unhelpful in patients with acute respiratory failure and a new diagnosis of fibrosing ILD. Severe hypoxia, respiratory failure, immunocompromised status and rapidly progressive disease prior to biopsy are associated with increased morbidity and mortality. Multidisciplinary discussion, utilizing the available clinical, HRCT and SLB data is useful in making a definitive diagnosis; however, diagnostic uncertainty may still occur in a minority of ILD patients.

Figure Legends


Figure 1
Suggested algorithm outlining the     MANUS CRIP T
                                      role of surgical lung biopsy in interstitial lung diseases.


Figure 2a, 2b
Usual Interstitial Pneumonitis pattern (UIP). Axial (Figure 2a) and coronal (Figure 2b) images
through the chest show peripheral predominant, basal predominant reticular abnormality with
small areas of honeycombing (arrows).


Figure 3a, 3b
Possible UIP pattern. Axial (Figure 3a) and coronal (Figure 3b) images through the chest show
peripheral predominant basal predominant reticular abnormality without honeycombing. Biopsy
showed UIP.


Figure 4a, 4b  ACCEP TED
Atypical UIP. Axial (Figure 4a) and coronal (Figure 4b) images through the chest in a patient
with scleroderma show basal predominant reticular and ground glass abnormality without
honeycombing. There is slight peripheral predominance, but the extent of ground glass
abnormality is greater than would be expected for UIP. However, biopsy showed UIP.


Figure 5a, 5b
Hypersensitivity pneumonitis. Axial (Figure 5a) and coronal (Figure 5b) images through the
chest show reticular and ground glass abnormality, with slight midlung predominance, and
relative sparing of the lung bases. There is a small amount of posterior subpleural honeycombing
on the axial image. Despite this finding, the distribution is inconsistent with UIP, and much more
suggestive of hypersensitivity pneumonitis. Biopsy showed hypersensitivity pneumonitis.


Figure 6a, 6b
(Figure 6a) UIP. A low power view of lung biopsy shows temporally and spatially heterogeneous
patchy (primarily subpleural) fibrosis with intervening areas of normal lung parenchyma. This
geographic heterogeneity can only be appreciated in a low power view of a SLB, not a TBBx.
